blocks.body: To-Infinitive helps you talk about intention, plan, purpose, decision, and many common verb patterns.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"In this lesson you will see how the to-infinitive helps you talk about plans, intentions, purpose, and the common patterns that appear after many verbs and adjectives.","rules":[{"label":"FORM","color":"emerald","text":"The to-infinitive has the pattern to + base verb: to go, to study, to help.","example":"I want to leave early."},{"label":"PURPOSE AND INTENTION","color":"blue","text":"This form often shows why we do something or what we plan to do.","example":"She called to ask a question."},{"label":"AFTER VERBS AND ADJECTIVES","color":"amber","text":"After many verbs and adjectives, the natural choice is the to-infinitive.","example":"decide to wait / happy to help"}]}

{"title":"1. Main form","intro":"The to-infinitive = to + base verb. This pattern often appears after a verb, after an adjective, or to show purpose.","items":[{"label":"Basic pattern","title":"to + V","subtitle":"to work / to read / to stay"},{"label":"Negative","title":"not to + V","subtitle":"She decided not to go."},{"label":"After an adjective","title":"adjective + to + V","subtitle":"happy to help / difficult to explain"},{"label":"Purpose","title":"to + V = for this purpose","subtitle":"I opened the app to check the time."}]}

{"title":"2. When to use the to-infinitive","sections":[{"label":"After common verbs","color":"emerald","description":"After verbs like want, hope, decide, plan, promise, refuse, the natural continuation is often the to-infinitive.","examples":[{"en":"We decided to stay at home.","ua":"We decided to stay at home."},{"en":"I hope to see you soon.","ua":"I hope to see you soon."},{"en":"She refused to answer the question.","ua":"She refused to answer the question."}]},{"label":"To show purpose","color":"blue","description":"The to-infinitive often explains why we do an action.","examples":[{"en":"He went outside to make a phone call.","ua":"He went outside to make a phone call."},{"en":"I am saving money to buy a laptop.","ua":"I am saving money to buy a laptop."},{"en":"She paused to read the message carefully.","ua":"She paused to read the message carefully."}],"note":"Here the to-infinitive often means “in order to do something”."},{"label":"After adjectives and in fixed patterns","color":"amber","description":"After many adjectives or patterns with too / enough, we also use the to-infinitive.","examples":[{"en":"I am glad to help.","ua":"I am glad to help."},{"en":"This text is easy to understand.","ua":"This text is easy to understand."},{"en":"The bag is too heavy to carry.","ua":"The bag is too heavy to carry."}],"note":"After an adjective, the sentence is often incomplete without the verb form that follows."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Leaving out to after a verb that takes the infinitive.","wrong":"I want go now.","right":"✅ I want to go now.","hint":"After want, the standard pattern is to + verb."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Not using the infinitive after an adjective when it is needed.","wrong":"It is important understand the rule.","right":"✅ It is important to understand the rule.","hint":"After important, easy, hard, happy, and many other adjectives, the to-infinitive is often needed."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Missing the purpose link between two actions.","wrong":"She went to the kitchen and make coffee.","right":"✅ She went to the kitchen to make coffee.","hint":"When the second action shows purpose, the to-infinitive is clearer and more natural."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["The to-infinitive = to + base verb.","We often use it after verbs like want, hope, decide, plan, promise, refuse.","The to-infinitive clearly shows purpose: to ask, to buy, to check.","After many adjectives, we also need a to-infinitive: happy to help, easy to read.","If the link to the next action is missing after a verb, check whether a to-infinitive is needed."]}
